explicit_text: I was relocated to the Amache camp in Colorado during the war. It was a difficult time for many Japanese Americans, including myself. Amache was one of the places where we were confined, but it was also where we shared our stories and supported each other. The experience there deeply affected my life and those of many others.

explicit_text: My name is Shigeru Mayeda, born in 1902 in Niigata Prefecture. I spent my early years in Hawaii before returning to Japan to study. Later, I came back to the United States, settling in Fresno and then Loomis, California, where I farmed. I was able to buy land due to my citizenship and later moved to Cortez, Colorado, where I raised my family and was active in the Cortez Christian Church.

I was able to buy land due to my citizenship and later moved to a community in southwestern Colorado, where I raised my family and was active in the local Christian church.

explicit_text: The United States has been my home for most of my life, though I was born in Japan. I came here as a child and later returned after studying in Japan. I farmed in various places like Fresno, Loomis, and Cortez, and faced many challenges including land ownership laws and the Great Depression. Despite hardships, I raised my family here and contributed to the community.

explicit_text: I moved to Fresno to go to school, where there were very few Japanese people at the time. It was a place where I had to learn to live among white Americans and improve my English. Later, my son moved to Fresno to work as a gardener before switching to stock trading. Fresno was an important place in our family's journey.

explicit_text: After returning to the U.S., I farmed in Loomis, California. It was during a time when Japanese people could not lease land, but since I was a citizen, I was able to buy and lease land. I even helped friends by letting them use my name to buy land. Eventually, I sold my land there because I was worried it might be illegal.

explicit_text: I moved to Cortez, Colorado, after buying about 20 acres of land from Mr. Abiko. Cortez was a small community then, with a small church where I was a member. I raised my three children there after my wife passed away. Cortez became our permanent home, and my daughter still lives there with her family.

explicit_text: World War I ended in 1918, and during the war, many Japanese farmers made money which helped them buy land in places like Cortez. The war's economic conditions allowed us to invest in land and settle down. However, after the war, we faced new challenges including the Great Depression.

explicit_text: The Great Depression hit us hard in the early 1930s. We struggled to pay mortgages on our land and could not sell our wine grapes due to Prohibition and economic downturn. Many families, including mine, had to grow fast vegetables to survive while waiting for grapes to mature. It was a time of great hardship and community support.

explicit_text: During the Great Depression, Herbert Hoover was president, and his policies affected us deeply. It was a tough time when banks closed and money was scarce. We struggled to pay our debts and keep our farms going under his administration.

explicit_text: Prohibition made it impossible for us to sell wine grapes legally, which was a major blow to our farming income. Even though we grew grapes, we couldn't market them as wine, and that contributed to our financial struggles during the Depression. It was a frustrating time for grape growers like us.
